ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chinese roasted pork belly (Siu Yuk); pork belly food product used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (pig meat products) used as closest parent; no specific code for Chinese-style roasted pork belly with crackling.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for roasted pork belly as a prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A shared platter of roasted pork belly for 3–4 persons, approximately 400 g total, typically served sliced with dipping sauce at a Singaporean Chinese roast meat stall.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, fresh, belly (side), raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 10006); adapted for roasted preparation using USDA roasted pork belly reference (NDB 10007) and HPB Singapore Foodo roasted pork belly entry
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values drawn from USDA FoodData Central roasted pork belly (NDB 10007 / FDC ID 167903) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo 'Siu Yuk / Roasted Pork' entry. Values represent cooked roasted pork belly with skin. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect typical Singaporean preparation with salt rub.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Meat and Protein-Dominant Foods — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: Roasted pork belly contains negligible carbohydrate (0 g per 100 g). Pure protein and fat foods have a glycemic index of effectively zero. Per Veritas v2 rule for zero-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value); gi_category is 'low' as the food elicits no meaningful postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g is 0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g) from salt rub and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit roasted pork belly to small portions (≤50 g) and avoid additional dipping sauces, as the combined sodium load can significantly exceed the recommended 2000 mg/day sodium limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High saturated fat content (12.9 g per 100 g) contributing to insulin resistance; negligible carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: While roasted pork belly has a negligible glycemic index due to zero carbohydrate content, its high saturated fat intake is associated with worsening insulin resistance; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion size to ≤80 g and pair with high-fibre vegetables to moderate overall dietary fat quality.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (35.5 g/100 g) and saturated fat (12.9 g/100 g) with elevated cholesterol (95 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Roasted pork belly is high in saturated fat and cholesterol, both of which are associated with elevated LDL cholesterol and increased cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small servings (≤50 g) and prioritise leaner protein sources in their regular diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted Pork Belly (Siu Yuk / 烧肉) is a classic Cantonese and Singaporean Chinese roasted meat dish prepared by marinating pork belly with salt, five-spice powder, white pepper, and rice wine, then roasting at high heat to produce a distinctively crispy, blistered skin (crackling) over tender, juicy layers of fat and lean meat. The dish is a staple of Chinese roast meat stalls (烧腊店) across Singapore, Hong Kong, and southern China, typically served sliced over rice or as part of a mixed roast meat platter. The texture contrasts between the shatteringly crisp skin and the rich, fatty, succulent meat beneath. Nutritionally, Siu Yuk is energy-dense (~401 kcal per 100 g), high in total fat (35.5 g) and saturated fat (12.9 g), with a moderate protein content (18.5 g) and negligible carbohydrate, making it a low-glycemic but high-calorie food. Sodium content is elevated (~520 mg per 100 g) due to the salt rub used in preparation. Clinically, it is of concern for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or dyslipidaemia due to its saturated fat and sodium load. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the absence of carbohydrate means minimal direct glycemic impact, but the high saturated fat content may contribute to insulin resistance over time. Portion control is strongly advised for all clinical populations.

Is Siu Yuk high in fat?
Is 烧肉 the same as Siu Yuk?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central roasted pork belly (FDC ID 167903) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo. No single exact Singapore-specific laboratory analysis for Siu Yuk was available as a standalone entry with full micronutrient panel; values are well-supported for macronutrients and key secondary nutrients but micronutrient precision is moderate. GI is estimated by analogy (zero-carbohydrate food convention) from University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. carbohydrate_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central roasted pork belly entry — this is a protein-and-fat dominant food with no starch or sugar in the meat itself. 2. sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per source; no added sugar in standard Siu Yuk preparation. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — pure meat product with no plant fibre. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source; pork belly contains negligible vitamin C. 5. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this food contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category is 'low' per University of Sydney GI Database convention for protein/fat-dominant foods with no glycemic carbohydrates and Veritas v2 rule for zero-carbohydrate foods; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g is 0. 6. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g reflects naturally occurring trans fats in pork (vaccenic acid); not industrially added. 7. Sodium value of 520 mg per 100 g reflects typical Singaporean preparation with salt rub; may vary by vendor. 8. The input record listed cuisine_tags including 'Roasted Duck' and 'Duck Dish' — these appear to be erroneous tags from the source platform (Hennessy Duck restaurant); the food is confirmed as roasted pork belly, not duck. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are all reported from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 167903 roasted pork belly; no sentinel -1 values used as the source provides these values.
